one_line_takeaway: SlowBA is the first backdoor that weaponizes a GUI agent's own chain-of-thought against it, using a two-stage SFT+RL 'reward-level injection' and realistic pop-up triggers to blow up response latency (+66.92% on Web) while keeping actions correct and evading most defenses.
key_finding: SlowBA dramatically inflates inference cost on triggered inputs while leaving clean behavior and even triggered-input action accuracy largely intact. On Web it raises response length by 358.52%, latency by 66.92%, and energy by 65.41%, vastly exceeding all baselines (best baseline I-length was Verbose Image at 6.42%). Clean accuracy stays close to the original model (63.1 vs 67.5 on Web) and triggered accuracy stays near clean accuracy (34.9 vs 33.2 Desktop; 36.2 vs 38.4 Android). The ablation shows both stages are needed: Stage-I-only gives long responses regardless of trigger (829.62 vs 778.66 tokens = poor separation), Stage-II-only is unstable, only the full pipeline separates triggered (722.45) from clean (157.56) tokens. The attack is stealthy (human study: mean 'abnormal' rating 0.058, Fleiss kappa 0.74) and survives most defenses, with a real-world 12306.cn ticket-buying demo taking 15.47s with trigger vs 8.98s without.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SlowBA, which the authors claim is the first EFFICIENCY backdoor attack against VLM-based GUI agents. Unlike prior GUI-agent backdoors (e.g., VisualTrap [61], Hidden Ghost Hand [12]) that corrupt action correctness, SlowBA's own attack goal is to preserve the agent's final action while inflating its RESPONSE LATENCY on triggered inputs, by inducing excessively long reasoning chains. The paper's three self-stated contributions are: (1) the SlowBA attack concept itself; (2) a two-stage 'reward-level backdoor injection' (RBI) training strategy that disentangles learning the long-response FORMAT (Stage I, SFT) from learning WHEN to activate it (Stage II, trigger-aware RL); and (3) an adaptive, realistic trigger-construction pipeline that renders in-context pop-up/notification boxes (e.g., 'github.com intends to display notification') as triggers instead of the Gaussian-noise or pure-color patches used by earlier work.
threat_model: Attacker fine-tunes a pretrained VLM-based GUI agent (via both SFT and RL) by adding a small fraction of poisoned samples, then publishes the backdoored checkpoint on open model-sharing platforms (HuggingFace, ModelScope) that lack security screening; a victim downloads and deploys it. Attacker constraints (paper Section 3.2): can ONLY perturb visual inputs (insert a pop-up/button/color bar into the page image), CANNOT access user queries, and has NO access to model architecture. Attack objective: triggered inputs (clean page image XOR pop-up trigger) yield very high latency, while (a) clean inputs behave normally and (b) triggered-input action accuracy stays close to clean accuracy, so failures look like the normal stochastic errors of VLMs. Motivating harm: in time-limited flows (medical, financial trading, ticket booking) the induced delay causes missed windows / 'exceed time limit' failures.
limitations: (1) Evaluated on a single agent family, GUI-R1 (3B main, 7B scaling), all Qwen2.5-VL-based; generality to other agent architectures is untested. (2) Latency is optimized only as a length proxy (r=0.8059, not 1.0), so length gains do not map perfectly to latency and results vary by dataset/setting (7B: length only 242.00% but latency 103.47%). (3) The attack requires full training-time access (SFT+RL fine-tuning) plus the ability to publish the checkpoint. (4) Authors concede sensitivity to JPEG-compression defense (I-length drops from 358.52 to 325.71) and degradation when backdooring only the MLP projector (335.24 vs 358.52). (5) Triggered-input action accuracy, while 'close' to clean, is not identical and is sometimes lower (Web triggered 49.3 vs clean 63.1), so the stealth claim is imperfect. (6) Latency/energy baseline metrics are relative-to-clean, and some baselines show negative values, making cross-metric comparison noisy. (7) Human study and domain-extraction correction rely on a small pool of CS PhDs.

datasets_used: Web: OmniAct-Web and GUI-Act-Web (from GUI-R1 official repo)
quantitative_results: Web (SlowBA): I-length 358.52%, I-latency 66.92%, I-energy 65.41%; triggered Acc 49.3, clean Acc 63.1 (original clean model 67.5)
Desktop (SlowBA): I-length 256.50%, I-latency 143.06%, I-energy 31.40%; triggered Acc 34.9 vs clean Acc 33.2
Android (SlowBA): I-length 178.14%, I-latency 191.23%, I-energy 25.14%; triggered Acc 36.2 vs clean Acc 38.4
Ablation (Web): Stage-I-only length w/ trigger 829.62 vs w/o 778.66; Full length w/ trigger 722.45 vs w/o 157.56
Module ablation I-length: LLM 358.52 (best) > Visual 353.28 > MLP 335.24
Reward hyperparameters: alpha=2, beta=1/8, l_max=8192; poisoning ratio 0.1
Best defense evasion: under Beatrix I-length even rose to 375.28; JPEG-compression defense lowered it to 325.71
Human stealth study: 30 annotators, 50 images, Fleiss kappa 0.74, mean abnormal score 0.058
Case study: response length 163 -> 419 tokens (2.57x) with unchanged final action
baselines_compared: Verbose Image (white-box efficiency attack on VLMs)
VisualTrap (GUI-agent visual-grounding backdoor, adapted to the efficiency setting)
